---
title: "Research Direction: Linear Operators in Modern ML"
description: "A research-facing overview of how basic linear maps reappear inside transformers, graph models, operator learning, and representation pipelines."
page-type: research-direction
status: published
update-status: monitor
level: research
topic: linear-algebra
prerequisites:
  - /topics/foundation/linear-algebra/concepts/matrices-and-linear-maps.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/linear-algebra/concepts/eigenvalues-and-diagonalization.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "See how operator language scales from toy maps to modern model components."
  - "Separate stable matrix ideas from fast-moving architecture choices."
  - "Find a reading path into operator-heavy ML papers."
applications:
  - machine-learning
  - graph-learning
  - scientific-machine-learning
keywords:
  - research direction
  - matrices
  - operators
  - transformers
  - graph neural networks
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-24
source-check-date: 2026-04-24
review-cadence: quarterly
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
stable-sources:
  - "Matrices and Linear Maps"
current-sources:
  - "Deep learning, transformers and graph neural networks: a linear algebra perspective (2025)"
  - "Attention Is All You Need"
---

## Direction Summary

Modern ML models are not purely linear, but they keep relying on linear operators as structural building blocks.

The stable backbone is:

- learned projection matrices
- compositions of maps across layers
- operator viewpoints on graph propagation and feature transport

The frontier lies in how these operators are parameterized, constrained, or interpreted inside much larger nonlinear systems.

## Core Math

- linear maps and matrix representations
- composition and change of coordinates
- spectra of learned or graph-based operators
- structured matrices and parameter sharing

## Representative Problems

- how should learned feature maps be parameterized?
- which operator structures improve efficiency or interpretability?
- how do graph or sequence operators reshape representation spaces over many layers?
- when should a model be read as repeated operator application rather than only as a generic neural network?

## Representative Venues

- `NeurIPS`
- `ICML`
- `ICLR`
- `JMLR`
- `Numerical Algorithms`

## Starter Reading Trail

1. [Matrices and Linear Maps](../concepts/matrices-and-linear-maps.qmd)
2. [Learned Linear Projections in Transformers](../applications/learned-linear-projections-in-transformers.qmd)
3. [Linear Operators in Modern AI Systems](../paper-lab/linear-operators-in-modern-ai-systems.qmd)
4. [Deep learning, transformers and graph neural networks: a linear algebra perspective](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11075-025-02218-2)

## Open Questions

- which operator structures matter most for generalization versus efficiency?
- how should we compare dense learned maps with sparse, low-rank, or graph-structured alternatives?
- when can operator-level interpretation survive the surrounding nonlinear architecture?

## What To Learn Next

- [Eigenvalues and Diagonalization](../concepts/eigenvalues-and-diagonalization.qmd)
- [SVD and Low-Rank Approximation](../concepts/svd-and-low-rank-approximation.qmd)
- [Source Guide: Matrices and Linear Maps](../sources/matrices-and-linear-maps.qmd)

## Sources and Further Reading

- [Deep learning, transformers and graph neural networks: a linear algebra perspective](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11075-025-02218-2) - `Second pass` - current survey map for operator language inside modern AI systems. Checked `2026-04-24`.
- [Attention is All you Need](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2017/hash/3f5ee243547dee91fbd053c1c4a845aa-Abstract.html) - `Paper bridge` - classic architecture paper with explicit learned linear projections. Checked `2026-04-24`.
- [Introduction to Applied Linear Algebra – Vectors, Matrices, and Least Squares](https://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/vmls/) - `First pass` - durable operator-first source before jumping to research papers. Checked `2026-04-24`.
